topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The polished mahogany of Dr. Alistair Thorne’s office gleamed under the soft lighting, a stark contrast to the chipped paint of the hallway where Mark used to corner me. I watched him now, leaning forward, his expression one of calm, measured understanding as he listened to a nervous young patient. My stomach clenched, a tight, unpleasant knot forming beneath my ribs. It wasn't just that he was successful; it was the effortless grace with which he handled people, the quiet authority that settled over the room like a warm blanket. I fiddled with the frayed edge of my sleeve, my gaze darting from his composed face to the stack of journals on his desk, a burning resentment rising in my throat. I wanted that level of acceptance, that kind of profound connection, and it was all emanating from the person who once made my every day a tightrope walk of anxiety.

[story 2]

I stood by the window, the city lights blurring into streaks of indifferent color. Across the street, I saw him—Mr. Thorne, impeccably dressed, radiating an aura of serene competence. A sharp, almost painful tightening gripped my chest. It felt like watching someone effortlessly swim in a pool I couldn't even approach. I clenched my fists inside my coat pockets, the knuckles turning white. Every time he offered a gentle nod to a visitor, a strange, acidic feeling washed over me. It was the recognition that he possessed an inherent magnetism, a soothing quality that I had spent years trying to manufacture through aggression. I turned away quickly, the sound of my own shallow breathing loud in the quiet apartment, wishing, with a sudden, sharp pang, that I could just absorb that kind of effortless composure.

[story 3]

"It's all about active listening, isn't it?" Mr. Thorne murmured, his voice a low, resonant balm. I sat on the edge of the visitor’s chair, my posture rigid, my eyes fixed on the way his hands moved as he gestured thoughtfully. A heat bloomed low in my abdomen, a restless, churning sensation that made me want to shift my weight constantly. I observed him, cataloging every subtle gesture, every perfectly modulated tone. It was infuriating. He had taken the raw, unfiltered energy I used to project as dominance and refined it into something utterly compelling. A bitter taste coated my tongue. I forced myself to look down at my hands, focusing on the faint tremor there, wishing I could somehow channel that same smooth, undeniable presence he commanded.
